content:
Welcome back to episode 18 of the ICT 2022 YouTube Mentorship. This episode focuses on the Euro
dollar. I will be demonstrating my trade layout and how I utilize TradingView charts. Please note, my specific
multi-chart layout may require a higher TradingView membership level, but you can achieve similar analysis
with individual charts on a single screen.
This lesson addresses common questions from both my private group and the YouTube community
regarding:
- My chart appearance during trade setup analysis
- Navigation from higher time frames to lower time frames
- Working with daily bias
- Streamlining analysis from high to low time frames

- IDENTIFYING BIAS AND FAIR VALUE GAPS
The upper left-hand corner of my layout always displays the daily chart. This is where your analysis for bias
should begin. You can perform this analysis with a maximized chart.
Referring to a previous midweek commentary, we observed a fair value gap (FVG) on the daily chart. It's
crucial to correctly identify market structures; for instance, a high, a low, and a higher high might be
mistaken for a bearish breaker, but it is not always the case. The key is to identify the lowest point relevant
to the FVG (e.g., the discount low of the FVG). I previously mentioned that price would likely draw up into
this FVG before looking for lower prices.

- EMBRACING IMPERFECTION AND STREAMLINED APPROACH
A common question is whether there's a specific trading approach that 'hits all the time.' There is no such
method. Everything I trade and teach is imperfect, meaning there will be losing transactions, largely due to
the operator (the trader). Taking ownership of this is crucial.
If I were to start completely over, knowing what I know, I would use the model presented in this 2022
YouTube Mentorship. This model is designed to be streamlined and step-by-step. I would not focus on
concepts like:
- Central Bank Dealer's Range
- Asian Range
- Any other concepts not specifically mentioned in this model
This model is the simplest way to work with intraday and daily order flow. It's designed to be straightforward
and easy to understand, even for beginners, avoiding unnecessary complexities.

- DAILY BIAS AND FOREX SPECIFICS
The purpose of streamlining analysis is to create a clear, step-by-step process. For the current example,
the daily bias is bearish for the Euro dollar. This means we are primarily looking for short sale setups. It's
important to note that everything discussed in this video, unless stated otherwise, is pertinent to Forex
trading.
With a bearish bias, we expect the market to draw lower. However, profitability doesn't require catching the
absolute lowest price. The goal is to responsibly handle daily bias, work with higher time frames, and frame
out a setup using the model's logic.

section_title: COMMITMENT TO BIAS AND REAL-WORLD TRADING IMPERFECTIONS
content:
When a bearish bias is established, the objective is to employ short sale ideas and filter out long trade
ideas. It's crucial to commit to one direction unless there's clear evidence that your bias is incorrect. A bias
is an idea to work within; it's not always perfect, and even I make errors in bias occasionally.
For new traders, it's vital to understand that there is no secret method to remove the chance of losing.
Nobody can teach a perfect, never-losing system because it doesn't exist. If such a system existed, its
owner would likely not teach it publicly. Imperfection in trading is normal, and it's possible to be profitable
even with a low win rate, as demonstrated by live account statements. These statements often reflect
specific teaching moments to help students overcome drawdown or fear of taking trades, not necessarily
showcasing perfect entries.

Regarding funded account programs, I have no affiliation or personal trading experience with them, but I
know students who have successfully passed their challenges and are funded. While I don't believe
everyone should pursue them, they are a viable avenue for some. If I were starting over with a funded
account, I would use this mentorship model and the process I'm about to demonstrate.

- RETRACEMENT AND PROFIT TARGETS
On the daily chart, after a significant drop (e.g., five consecutive down days), a retracement is likely. We
identify a fair value gap (FVG) and understand that the market is in a discount range, expecting a rally into
the FVG (a premium level) before continuing lower.
- Highlight the FVG on your daily chart.
- Identify logical targets. While a lower low (draw on liquidity) is the ultimate daily target, it's not necessarily
your intraday trade target.
- Frame your daily highs and lows. Institutional traders and high-frequency trading algorithms often target
previous daily highs and lows due to the liquidity accumulated there. Marking the last three days' highs and
lows provides a fresh supply of setups.
For our example, after Wednesday's trading, we identify a premium level (the FVG's high) and a discount
level (Wednesday's low). The expectation for Thursday's trading is for price to open, rally up into the
premium FVG, and then sell off, targeting Wednesday's daily low. This offers a respectable profit range
(e.g., 60 handles or pips).

section_title: MULTI-TIMEFRAME CHART LAYOUT AND TRADING PLATFORM BEST PRACTICES
content:
My preferred multi-timeframe layout on TradingView is:
- Upper Left: Daily chart
- Lower Left: Hourly chart
- Right (largest screen): 15-minute chart
This setup allows annotations made on one chart to transpose to others, which is a useful feature of
TradingView. If you're working with a single screen (e.g., a laptop), you can perform annotations on the
daily, then switch to the hourly, then the 15-minute, and the annotations will carry over.
Regarding trading devices:
- Avoid entering new positions on a phone. You cannot see enough data, and the view is too compressed
for proper analysis.
- Managing an existing trade on a phone is acceptable.
- For serious analysis and entry, a computer screen is essential. Investing in a proper setup, even just one
monitor, is a necessary investment.

- DAILY DELINEATION AND MIDNIGHT OPEN
On the 15-minute chart, we implement specific annotations:
- Draw a vertical line to delineate a new day at midnight New York local time. It is critical that your
TradingView chart is set to 'New York' time zone for consistency with my teachings.
- Highlight the opening price at New York midnight. Draw a horizontal line from this price, extending it to 11
AM New York local time. This line represents the daily opening price and serves as a key reference.
All annotations made on higher timeframes (daily, hourly) will automatically appear on the 15-minute chart
if your TradingView charts are linked (using the 'link' button).

- DISPLACEMENT AND ENTRY SETUP
With the daily bias established as bearish, we are looking for specific conditions on the 15-minute chart:
- Price rallies above relative equal highs, fulfilling the higher timeframe (daily) fair value gap (FVG)
requirement.
- After hitting this premium level, we look for displacement to the downside, breaking a swing low. This
indicates an internal shift in market structure to bearish.
- Identify the new fair value gap created after this displacement.
We then focus on the New York Kill Zone for Forex, which is typically 7:00 AM to 10:00 AM New York local
time. This is the window where we expect the setup to form. If price enters the newly formed FVG within
this kill zone, that presents an opportunity for a short entry. Your limit order would be placed at or just above
the FVG's entry point, with a stop loss placed above the previous swing high, or above the highest point of
the FVG if the range is large.

section_title: FINE-TUNING ENTRY WITH 5-MINUTE CHART AND CORRECT ORDER BLOCK
content:
PRINCIPLES
To fine-tune entries, we can drop down to the 5-minute chart. The higher time frame imbalances (parent
imbalances) are the dominant ones. The 5-minute chart will show more refined fair value gaps within the
larger 15-minute FVG.
- You can place a sell limit order at the minimum expectation (the lowest threshold of the FVG on the 5
- minute chart) to ensure a fill, though this might involve higher initial risk (in pips).
- Alternatively, you can aim for a 'better' entry deeper into the FVG or a higher FVG, but this increases the
chance of missing the trade.
Understanding Order Blocks:
- A true order block is a consecutive run of candles that leads to a displacement, and crucially, it must have
an imbalance (fair value gap) after it to be valid. Without an imbalance, there is no order block. This
differentiates it from supply and demand zones, which often require 'fresh' zones and don't involve
imbalances in the same way.
- The misconception that an order block is always the 'last up-closed candle before the down move' (for a
bearish order block) is incorrect. The higher time frame parent imbalance dictates where price needs to
trade into, not necessarily the last candle. If the parent imbalance is satisfied before reaching that last
candle, it's not required to trade further.

section_title: ORDER BLOCK APPLICATION, STOP LOSS, AND KILL ZONE EXIT STRATEGY
content:
Continuing on the 5-minute chart, after observing the run into the daily FVG and above the hourly relative
equal highs, we identify the bearish order block. This is the consecutive run of up-closed candles that
initiates the move into the higher timeframe imbalance, followed by a displacement and a fair value gap.
- The critical element for an order block's validity is the subsequent fair value gap (imbalance). If price
rallies into this order block and fair value gap, the trade becomes viable.
- Stop Loss Placement: Your stop loss can be placed above the intermediate-term swing high of the order
block, as price should ideally not trade higher than that if the bearish premise holds.
- Entry Strategy: Place a limit order at a conservative entry point within the fair value gap to avoid missing
the trade, even if it means slightly higher initial risk in pips. Remember, actual dollar risk is controlled by lot
size, not just pip count.
- Kill Zone Management: If your limit order is not filled by 11:30 AM New York local time, pull the order. Do
not leave 'good till cancel' orders for intraday setups like this. Kill zones define the period when you should
be determining and placing your order, not necessarily when it must be filled. If no setup is derived by the
end of the kill zone, you simply wait for the next opportunity.

- 40 PIPS EXAMPLE
In our example, the market breaks lower after the setup, and the target (the previous day's low) is reached
and filled around 2:45 PM. This demonstrates a 40-pip gain. It highlights that you don't need to catch the
absolute highest high for a profitable trade; the structured approach is what matters.
This framework, logic, and the elements taught in this YouTube mentorship demonstrate how markets are
delivered algorithmically. These concepts are observable and repeatable, countering arguments that such
patterns don't exist.

section_title: IMPORTANCE OF CONSISTENT CHART WORK AND STUDY JOURNALS
content:
The key to success with this information is consistent work:
- Go through your charts and annotate them daily, just as demonstrated.
- Maintain a study journal, logging your observations, potential setups, and any questions or further
investigation points.
- At the end of the week, review your entire week's price action, connecting intraday movements to daily
and weekly charts. Analyze when highs/lows were formed, the biggest movement days, and how
everything fit together.
This process is like creating your own, personalized technical analysis book. By studying past price action
(hindsight), you train your mind to recognize these repeating market signatures in real-time. This develops
your psychological makeup as a trader and helps you trust the model. Do not be discouraged by those who
criticize studying hindsight; it is fundamental to learning in any complex field. Furthermore, I recommend
stopping buying conventional trading books, as many contain rehashed, flawed logic.

section_title: DEVELOPING TECHNICAL ANALYST SKILLS AND FINAL REMARKS
content:
Years of consistent study journal work will astonish you with how proficient you become as a technical
analyst. You will begin to see many more setups, even the subtle ones, that are not initially obvious. While
every day's price action won't be identical, the underlying 'signatures' or patterns are generally very close
and repeatable. You'll recognize them like identifying bear prints of different sizes in the snow.
Note on Gold Trading: Gold is an event-driven and highly manipulated market, often involving many stop
hunts and consolidations. It is generally not recommended for aggressive short-term trading for those just
learning these concepts. There are other markets that offer better consistency and longevity for beginners.
My goal is to provide clear, palatable instruction for new viewers, showing that there is a logical, rhyming
reason behind market movements. Your job is to diligently apply these teachings and build your own
understanding through consistent chart work. This will equip you with the knowledge to trust price action
and achieve longevity in trading.
